notes: Sixth validated clue batch compiled from official school websites.
Clues intentionally avoid revealing enrollment size, grade span, or directional/proximity information because those are gameplay feedback channels.

clues.text: Its mission says the school promotes students' abilities to think and create in personally meaningful ways through an inquiry-based, integrated curriculum in a nurturing and empowering environment.
Its about page describes the school as cultivating curiosity since 2000.
The school defines inquiry as a rigorous discovery process driven by curriculum and curiosity that requires exploration, analysis, and problem-solving.
Official beliefs state that the whole child is more important than any single factor.
The board roster lists Dana Stachowiak as president and Mia Green as executive director ex officio.
Its campus street address is Wonder Way.
Its mission says the school pursues excellence in the holistic education of mind, body, and spirit.
The school says it prepares students for successful lifelong learning, social responsibility, environmental stewardship, and service.
Official materials say the school is grounded in EL Education standards for student achievement.
Its vision emphasizes an academically challenging, developmentally appropriate, experiential, holistic, child-centered education offered in partnership with parents, elders, and community.
The board page says the board meets on the third Thursday of each month.
Official pages list Jen Watkins as Executive Director and Ashley Shores-Bobowski as board chair.
Its mission says the Summit community engages students in learning experiences that stimulate discovery, inspire excellence, and nurture a positive influence in an ever-changing world.
Every morning, students recite a pledge built around seven virtues: Honesty, Respect, Responsibility, Compassion, Self-Discipline, Perseverance, and Giving.
Official pages say the curriculum uses a unique place-based approach that extends learning beyond the traditional classroom and into the outdoors and the community.
The campus page highlights features including a model Appalachian Trail, an amphitheater, and a climbing tower.
An expansion announcement says the school was established as K-8 in 1997 and was one of the first charter schools in North Carolina.
The trustee board page says board meetings are generally held on the third Tuesday of each month at 4:30 p.m.
Its vision statement is "Creating Navigators for Life."
Its mission says the school fosters creativity, confidence, and enthusiasm for learning through arts integration, family engagement, and a responsive academic and social curriculum.
The school says its social curriculum emphasizes the Responsive Classroom C.A.R.E.S. values: Cooperation, Assertion, Responsibility, Empathy, and Self-control.
Its origin story says the school was established by a group of parents and teachers in 1993 and later received charter status in 1998.
The board page says meetings are typically held on the third Tuesday of each month and begin at 6:00 p.m.
DeAnne Rosen is listed as Executive Director and Jonathan Bullock as board president.
Its mission says the school fosters curiosity and independence to develop learners into compassionate, responsible global citizens.
Its values include Inclusivity, Individual Growth, Interdependence, Peaceful Cooperation, and Respectful Care.
The school says students learn in mixed-age classrooms and have individualized work plans that allow choice in pace and order of work.
Official descriptions say the campus includes dedicated outdoor learning spaces for each classroom on a 17-acre site.
The board page says the Charter School Board typically meets monthly at 7 p.m. on the second Thursday.
Susan Johnstone is listed as Head of School, and her bio says she was both a founding board member and a founding teacher in 2013.
